subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 2 periods = 80 minutes
Total: 2 periods · 1h 20min

periods.activity_title: The Invisible Chair: Reading and Discovering True Friends

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Find friendship words in word-search grid
Mid-story: True/False, attic question, and prediction
End-story: True/False, fill blank, match characters to actions

phases.description: Students quickly write two words that come to mind when they think of friendship, then scan the word-search grid on page 59 to find eight friendship-related words. Pairs share their found words with the class before reading begins.

Teacher reads 'The Chair' aloud to the mid-story pause, then poses the questions on page 61: students respond to the True/False statement about Mario's pride, identify what grandfather brought from the attic, and predict whether Mario will face challenges with the invisible chair.

Teacher reads to the end of the story. Students individually answer the end-of-story questions on page 64: True/False on Mario's first attempt, filling in the blank about the bet, and matching Guneet/Asma/Deepa and the rest of the class to their actions.

Class discussion: what is the grandfather's real lesson? Students summarise in one sentence. Teacher previews the extract-based passage questions as self-study and introduces the writing and grammar periods ahead.

periods.teacher_notes: Transition from Section B into the final and longest narrative section. The prose_summary notes that the invisible chair is a metaphor for the invisible tests of loyalty — draw this out in the end-of-story discussion. Extract-based passage tasks (task_index 6, 7, 8) are left as self-study; direct students to pages 64–65.

periods.activity_title: Suffixes, Adjectives, and Friendship Across Ages

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Split six words into root word and suffix in table
Write adjectives around Mario's image to describe his character
Match adjectives to nouns; form phrases and sentences
Interview four age groups on friendship; record and present findings

phases.description: Teacher introduces root words and suffixes using 'friendly = friend + ly' from the story text, then students complete the table on page 65 splitting suddenly, talking, carrying, called, and showed. Pairs check and justify their splits.

Teacher reads aloud 'Mario, brave and determined, took the strange invisible chair…' and briefly explains that words describing people or things are adjectives. Students write adjectives around the image of Mario on page 66, drawing on both story vocabulary and their own words.

Students match adjectives (brave, clever, tasty, heavy, slight, true) with nouns from Column B on page 67, form phrases, and write one sentence each. Three or four students share their sentences aloud for peer feedback.

Teacher introduces the age-group interview project on page 70: students plan which four age groups they will speak to, practise the question 'What does friendship mean to you?', and begin filling in the response table. Students discuss in groups what patterns they expect to find across age groups.

Chapter close: teacher draws together the three sections — Gajaraj and Buntee, the prayer-poem, and Mario's invisible chair — asking what one idea about friendship all three share. Students give a one-word answer. Acrostic poem task (page 70) is recommended as a self-study creative extension.

periods.teacher_notes: Final period of the chapter; vocabulary_grammar and beyond_text are combined because the beyond-text project requires only planning and launching in-class — data collection is done independently. Task_index 1 (root-suffix matching with sentences) and task_index 4 (adjective-noun paragraph completion) from vocabulary_grammar are left as self-study; direct students to pages 66–67.

items.source_lo: Student analyses character motivation and distinguishes true friends from acquaintances.
items.item_stem: In 'The Chair', Mario's grandfather challenges him with a wager and produces an invisible chair from the attic. When Mario takes the chair to school, he falls repeatedly while most classmates laugh. However, Guneet, Asma, and Deepa silently move behind him and hold him up on his final attempt. Why does the grandfather describe the chair as 'special'? What does the chair reveal about the difference between Mario's true friends and his other classmates? Write your answer in 3–4 sentences.

expected_elements: The chair is 'special' because it reveals who genuinely cares for Mario.
True friends (Guneet, Asma, Deepa) act to help rather than mock his failure.
Other classmates are mere companions — they laugh at Mario's bad luck.
The grandfather's lesson: real friends never take joy in your misfortune.

items.question_text: In 'The Chair', Mario's grandfather challenges him with a wager and produces an invisible chair from the attic. When Mario takes the chair to school, he falls repeatedly while most classmates laugh. However, Guneet, Asma, and Deepa silently move behind him and hold him up on his final attempt. Why does the grandfather describe the chair as 'special'? What does the chair reveal about the difference between Mario's true friends and his other classmates? Write your answer in 3–4 sentences.

items.source_lo: Student listens to a personal account and identifies key facts about a friendship.
items.item_stem:
You will listen to Manu talk about her friend Monika and how they became friends. After listening, decide whether each statement below is TRUE or FALSE.
(1) Manu found it easy to adjust when she first joined the new school.
(2) Monika introduced Manu to other students and shared her notes with her.
(3) Manu and Monika have only experienced happy times together since meeting.
(4) Manu is grateful to Monika for her help and friendship.

options.text: Manu found it easy to adjust when she first joined the new school.
Monika introduced Manu to other students and shared her notes with her.
Manu and Monika have only experienced happy times together since meeting.
Manu is grateful to Monika for her help and friendship.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. False — Manu says things were very difficult at first. 2. True — Monika guided her and shared notes. 3. False — they have been together through both difficult and happy times. 4. True — Manu explicitly says she is thankful to Monika.

items.question_text:
You will listen to Manu talk about her friend Monika and how they became friends. After listening, decide whether each statement below is TRUE or FALSE.
(1) Manu found it easy to adjust when she first joined the new school.
(2) Monika introduced Manu to other students and shared her notes with her.
(3) Manu and Monika have only experienced happy times together since meeting.
(4) Manu is grateful to Monika for her help and friendship.

items.source_lo: Student applies adjectives to describe character and forms adjective-noun phrases.
items.item_stem:
In 'The Chair', Mario is described as brave and determined when he takes the invisible chair to school, and Guneet, Asma, and Deepa show that they are caring and supportive. Read the sentences below. Each sentence has a blank. Choose the most suitable adjective from the box to complete each sentence, then identify the noun that the adjective describes.

Adjective box: determined, invisible, generous, boastful, silent

1. Mario made a _____________ effort to sit on the chair even after falling several times.
2. The grandfather produced a _____________ chair from the attic to test Mario's friends.
3. Mario was _____________ about the number of friends he had at school.
4. The three friends made a _____________ move to stand behind Mario without drawing attention.
5. The grandfather was _____________ in offering fruit chaat to all four children that evening.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. determined (describes effort); 2. invisible (describes chair); 3. boastful (describes Mario); 4. silent (describes move); 5. generous (describes grandfather).

items.source_lo: Student investigates how perspectives on friendship change across age groups.
items.item_stem: Mario's grandfather uses the invisible chair to show Mario the difference between real friends and mere acquaintances. Different people at different stages of life may understand friendship very differently. Plan and carry out a short survey: ask at least ONE person from each of the following age groups the question 'What does friendship mean to you?' — Group 1: age 12–19; Group 2: age 20–30; Group 3: age 31–49; Group 4: age 50 and above. Record their responses in the table below. Then, in 3–4 sentences, describe ONE similarity and ONE difference you noticed across the age groups and explain what this tells you about friendship.
items.visual_stimulus:
Age Group|Person Interviewed|Their Response
12–19| | 
20–30| | 
31–49| | 
50 and above| |

expected_elements: Table is filled with actual responses from four distinct age groups.
Identifies one genuine similarity across at least two age groups.
Identifies one meaningful difference in how friendship is understood.
Reflection explains what the contrast reveals about life experience and friendship.

items.question_text: Mario's grandfather uses the invisible chair to show Mario the difference between real friends and mere acquaintances. Different people at different stages of life may understand friendship very differently. Plan and carry out a short survey: ask at least ONE person from each of the following age groups the question 'What does friendship mean to you?' — Group 1: age 12–19; Group 2: age 20–30; Group 3: age 31–49; Group 4: age 50 and above. Record their responses in the table below. Then, in 3–4 sentences, describe ONE similarity and ONE difference you noticed across the age groups and explain what this tells you about friendship.
